sections.title: Cloud computing service providers under oversight framework
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Oversight Framework established by the Regulation must cover cloud computing service providers where no Union horizontal framework for digital oversight exists. Financial entities require comprehensive ICT risk management capabilities, incident reporting mechanisms, third-party risk policies, and testing protocols. The framework must be proportionate for microenterprises and entities under simplified ICT risk management, while ensuring supervisory activities focus on critical ICT risks without duplication across Member States.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires oversight of cloud providers to ensure ICT risk management, incident reporting, and third-party risk controls for cross-border data transfers in financial services. Critical for DTIA due to cloud data residency and security implications.

sections.title: Exemptions for small financial entities from ICT risk management requirements
sections.summary: This section outlines exemptions for small and micro-enterprise financial entities from comprehensive ICT risk management obligations. It specifies that entities with fewer than 15 members, small investment firms, occupational retirement providers with under 100 members, and those exempted under sectoral directives should be subject to a simplified ICT risk management framework instead of the full requirements. The exemptions aim to balance proportionality with sector-specific regulatory needs.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines exemptions for small financial entities from full ICT risk management requirements, allowing them to use simplified frameworks that reduce compliance burdens while maintaining essential digital resilience measures.

sections.title: Reporting requirements for major ICT-related incidents
sections.summary: This section mandates that financial entities report major ICT-related incidents directly to their national competent authorities, with specific provisions for credit institutions classified as significant. It requires harmonized reporting channels, coordination with public authorities, and streamlined processes to ensure timely information sharing while maintaining data integrity and security. The reporting framework aims to enhance incident response and prevent systemic risks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes mandatory reporting of major ICT incidents to national authorities, enabling coordinated responses and enhanced digital resilience across financial entities while ensuring data integrity and security.

sections.title: ICT Third-Party Risk Management for Financial Entities
sections.summary: This section mandates financial entities to conduct thorough risk assessments for ICT third-party service providers, particularly those supporting critical functions. It requires supervisory approval, conflict of interest checks, and periodic rotation of testers. Financial entities retain full responsibility for compliance, must implement proportionate monitoring based on service criticality, and maintain detailed registers of ICT dependencies. The regulation explicitly excludes central banks and public authorities from oversight when providing ICT services for state functions.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial entities must identify and monitor ICT third-party dependencies, especially for critical functions, to prevent systemic risks. Requires detailed contractual terms, risk registers, and oversight mechanisms for cross-border data flows through third parties.

Financial entities must include provisions for data recovery in case of insolvency or business discontinuation
ICT third-party service providers must provide assistance in case of ICT incidents at no additional cost

sections.source_quote: In order to take advantage of internal resources available at corporate level, this Regulation should allow the use of internal testers for the purposes of carrying out TLPT, provided there is supervisory approval, no conflicts of interest, and periodical alternation of the use of internal and external testers (every three tests), while also requiring the provider of the threat intelligence in the TLPT to always be external to the financial entity.

sections.title: Cloud computing oversight for critical ICT service providers
sections.summary: This section mandates that critical ICT third-party service providers supplying financial services must maintain a Union subsidiary within 12 months of designation to enable effective oversight. The Lead Overseer must conduct monitoring missions to assess risk controls and potential impacts on financial stability. Providers must comply with transparency and access obligations, and failure to cooperate risks sanctions. The oversight framework requires critical providers to allow inspections and address Lead Overseer concerns to protect financial system integrity.
sections.dtia_summary: Critical ICT service providers must establish a Union subsidiary within 12 months of designation to ensure oversight effectiveness. This impacts cross-border data processing by requiring physical presence in the EU for critical providers, though data storage is not mandated.

sections.source_quote: Critical ICT third-party service providers, providing services to financial entities that affect the supply of financial services, should be required to maintain an adequate business presence in the Union. Due to the nature of the oversight, and the absence of comparable arrangements in other jurisdictions, there are no suitable alternative mechanisms ensuring this objective by way of effective cooperation with financial supervisors in third countries in relation to the monitoring of the impact of digital operational risks posed by systemic ICT third-party service providers, qualifying as critical ICT third-party service providers established in third countries.

sections.title: Cloud computing service oversight framework requirements
sections.summary: This section mandates that European Supervisory Authorities (ESAs) submit regulatory and implementing technical standards to the European Commission for adoption of delegated acts that carry over and update existing ICT risk management rules. It requires amendments to clarify that ICT risk-related provisions are laid down in this Regulation, and narrows the scope of relevant articles to include digital operational resilience aspects. The section also addresses systemic cyber risks in payment systems and recommends Member States draw inspiration from this Regulation's digital operational resilience requirements when supervising payment systems.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes requirements for cloud computing service oversight frameworks that impact data transfer by mandating technical standards for ICT risk management. It requires harmonized digital resilience rules for payment systems, which may affect cross-border data transfers involving financial entities.

sections.title: Exclusion of entities from GDPR scope by Member States
sections.summary: Member States can exclude financial entities listed in Article 2(5) points (4) to (23) of Directive 2013/36/EU from GDPR's scope if they are located within the Member State. When excluding such entities, Member States must notify the European Commission and provide updates on any changes to the exclusions. The Commission then publishes this information publicly. This section lists entities exempt from GDPR's scope under Article 2(5) points (4) to (23). It defines key financial terms like 'insurance undertaking', 'reinsurance undertaking', and references relevant EU directives and regulations for collective investment and prudential requirements. These exclusions apply to financial institutions and entities covered by specific EU legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows Member States to exclude specific financial entities from GDPR's scope, which may impact data transfer assessments for entities that are exempt from the regulation's requirements. sections.title: Definitions of financial sector terms for GDPR scope exclusions
sections.summary: This section defines specific terms used to exclude certain entities from GDPR's scope. It provides precise definitions for insurance intermediaries, reinsurance intermediaries, occupational retirement institutions, credit rating agencies, crypto-asset service providers, and other financial entities. These definitions are critical for determining which organizations fall outside the GDPR's applicability.

requirements.text: ‘insurance intermediary' means an insurance intermediary as defined in Article 2(1), point (3), of Directive (EU) 2016/97
‘ancillary insurance intermediary' means an ancillary insurance intermediary as defined in Article 2(1), point (4), of Directive (EU) 2016/97
‘reinsurance intermediary' means a reinsurance intermediary as defined in Article 2(1), point (5), of Directive (EU) 2016/97
‘institution for occupational retirement provision' means an institution for occupational retirement provision as defined in Article 6, point (1), of Directive (EU) 2016/2341
‘small institution for occupational retirement provision' means an institution for occupational retirement provision which operates pension schemes which together have less than 100 members in total
‘credit rating agency' means a credit rating agency as defined in Article 3(1), point (b), of Regulation (EC) No 1060/2009
‘crypto-asset service provider' means a crypto-asset service provider as defined in the relevant provision of the Regulation on markets in crypto-assets
‘issuer of asset-referenced tokens' means an issuer of asset-referenced tokens as defined in the relevant provision of the Regulation on markets in crypto-assets
‘administrator of critical benchmarks' means an administrator of ‘critical benchmarks' as defined in Article 3(1), point (25), of Regulation (EU) 2016/1011
‘crowdfunding service provider' means a crowdfunding service provider as defined in Article 2(1), point (e), of Regulation (EU) 2020/1503
‘securitisation repository' means a securitisation repository as defined in Article 2, point (23), of Regulation (EU) 2017/2402

‘microenterprise' means a financial entity, other than a trading venue, a central counterparty, a trade repository or a central securities depository, which employs fewer than 10 persons and has an annual turnover and/or annual balance sheet total that does not exceed EUR 2 million
‘Lead Overseer' means the European Supervisory Authority appointed in accordance with Article 31(1), point (b) of this Regulation
‘Joint Committee' means the committee referred to in Article 54 of Regulations (EU) No 1093/2010, (EU) No 1094/2010 and (EU) No 1095/2010

‘small enterprise' means a financial entity that employs 10 or more persons, but fewer than 50 persons, and has an annual turnover and/or annual balance sheet total that exceeds EUR 2 million, but does not exceed EUR 10 million

‘medium-sized enterprise' means a financial entity that is not a small enterprise and employs fewer than 250 persons and has an annual turnover that does not exceed EUR 50 million and/or an annual balance sheet that does not exceed EUR 43 million
‘public authority' means any government or other public administration entity, including national central banks

sections.source_quote: ‘insurance intermediary' means an insurance intermediary as defined in Article 2(1), point (3), of Directive (EU) 2016/97 of the European Parliament and of the Council (34);

sections.title: Risk assessment criteria for critical ICT service providers
sections.summary: Competent authorities must consider specific risk criteria when deciding whether to suspend or terminate critical ICT third-party services. These criteria include the severity of non-compliance, whether it reveals serious weaknesses, if financial crime was facilitated, intent behind non-compliance, business continuity risks, and opinions from designated authorities. Financial entities must be given time to adjust contracts and deploy exit strategies.

sections.source_quote: Upon receiving the reports referred to in Article 35(1), point (c), competent authorities, when taking a decision as referred to in paragraph 6 of this Article, shall take into account the type and magnitude of risk that is not addressed by the critical ICT third-party service provider, as well as the seriousness of the non-compliance, having regard to the following criteria: (a) the gravity and the duration of the non-compliance; (b) whether the non-compliance has revealed serious weaknesses in the critical ICT third-party service provider's procedures, management systems, risk management and internal controls; (c) whether a financial crime was facilitated, occasioned or is otherwise attributable to the non-compliance; (d) whether the non-compliance has been intentional or negligent; (e) whether the suspension or termination of the contractual arrangements introduces a risk for continuity of the financial entity's business operations notwithstanding the financial entity's efforts to avoid disruption in the provision of its services; (f) where applicable, the opinion of the competent authorities designated or established in accordance with Directive (EU) 2022/2555 responsible for the supervision of an essential or important entity subject to that Directive, which has been designated as a critical ICT third-party service provider, requested on a voluntary basis in accordance with paragraph 5 of this Article. Competent authorities shall grant financial entities the necessary period of time to enable them to adjust the contractual arrangements with critical ICT third-party service providers in order to avoid detrimental effects on their digital operational resilience and to allow them to deploy exit strategies and transition plans as referred to in Article 28.

sections.title: Risk assessment criteria for critical ICT service providers
sections.summary: Competent authorities must consider specific risk criteria when deciding whether to suspend or terminate critical ICT third-party services. These criteria include the severity of non-compliance, whether it reveals serious weaknesses, if financial crime was facilitated, intent behind non-compliance, business continuity risks, and opinions from designated authorities. Financial entities must be given time to adjust contracts and deploy exit strategies.

sections.source_quote: Upon receiving the reports referred to in Article 35(1), point (c), competent authorities, when taking a decision as referred to in paragraph 6 of this Article, shall take into account the type and magnitude of risk that is not addressed by the critical ICT third-party service provider, as well as the seriousness of the non-compliance, having regard to the following criteria: (a) the gravity and the duration of the non-compliance; (b) whether the non-compliance has revealed serious weaknesses in the critical ICT third-party service provider's procedures, management systems, risk management and internal controls; (c) whether a financial crime was facilitated, occasioned or is otherwise attributable to the non-compliance; (d) whether the non-compliance has been intentional or negligent; (e) whether the suspension or termination of the contractual arrangements introduces a risk for continuity of the financial entity's business operations notwithstanding the financial entity's efforts to avoid disruption in the provision of its services; (f) where applicable, the opinion of the competent authorities designated or established in accordance with Directive (EU) 2022/2555 responsible for the supervision of an essential or important entity subject to that Directive, which has been designated as a critical ICT third-party service provider, requested on a voluntary basis in accordance with paragraph 5 of this Article. Competent authorities shall grant financial entities the necessary period of time to enable them to adjust the contractual arrangements with critical ICT third-party service providers in order to avoid detrimental effects on their digital operational resilience and to allow them to deploy exit strategies and transition plans as referred to in Article 28.

sections.title: Exclusion of entities from GDPR scope by Member States
sections.summary: Member States can exclude financial entities listed in Article 2(5) points (4) to (23) of Directive 2013/36/EU from GDPR's scope if they are located within the Member State. When excluding such entities, Member States must notify the European Commission and provide updates on any changes to the exclusions. The Commission then publishes this information publicly. This section lists entities exempt from GDPR's scope under Article 2(5) points (4) to (23). It defines key financial terms like 'insurance undertaking', 'reinsurance undertaking', and references relevant EU directives and regulations for collective investment and prudential requirements. These exclusions apply to financial institutions and entities covered by specific EU legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows Member States to exclude specific financial entities from GDPR's scope, which may impact data transfer assessments for entities that are exempt from the regulation's requirements. This section identifies financial entities that may be excluded from GDPR's scope, which is relevant for DTIAs as it clarifies which entities require specific data transfer safeguards and which may operate under different regulatory frameworks.

sections.title: Delegated acts require no objections for entry into force
sections.summary: A delegated act adopted under Articles 31(6) and 43(2) enters into force only if no objections are expressed by either the European Parliament or Council within three months of notification. If no objections are made, the act enters into force; otherwise, it does not. The three-month period may be extended by either body.

sections.title: Non-binding opinions for consistent supervisory measures
sections.summary: If a critical ICT third-party service provider refuses to endorse recommendations from the Lead Overseer and this divergence risks financial stability, the Lead Overseer may issue non-binding opinions after consulting the Oversight Forum. These opinions aim to promote consistent supervisory follow-up measures across competent authorities to address potential risks to financial stability.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows the Lead Overseer to issue non-binding opinions to ensure consistent supervisory measures when critical ICT service providers refuse recommendations, affecting cross-border data processing and financial stability.

requirements.text: Lead Overseer may issue non-binding and non-public opinions to competent authorities
Opinions must be issued after consulting the Oversight Forum
Opinions aim to promote consistent and convergent supervisory follow-up measures

sections.source_quote: Where a critical ICT third-party service provider refuses to endorse recommendations, based on a divergent approach from the one advised by the Lead Overseer, and such a divergent approach may adversely impact a large number of financial entities, or a significant part of the financial sector, and individual warnings issued by competent authorities have not resulted in consistent approaches mitigating the potential risk to financial stability, the Lead Overseer may, after consulting the Oversight Forum, issue non-binding and non-public opinions to competent authorities, in order to promote consistent and convergent supervisory follow-up measures, as appropriate.

sections.title: Risk assessment criteria for critical ICT service providers
sections.summary: Competent authorities must consider specific risk criteria when deciding whether to suspend or terminate critical ICT third-party services. These criteria include the severity of non-compliance, whether it reveals serious weaknesses, if financial crime was facilitated, intent behind non-compliance, business continuity risks, and opinions from designated authorities. Financial entities must be given time to adjust contracts and deploy exit strategies.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details risk assessment criteria for critical ICT service providers, impacting data transfer and processing decisions for financial entities across borders.

requirements.text: Competent authorities shall take into account the type and magnitude of risk not addressed by critical ICT third-party service providers
Competent authorities shall consider gravity and duration of non-compliance
Competent authorities shall assess whether non-compliance reveals serious weaknesses in procedures, management systems, risk management, and internal controls
Competent authorities shall evaluate if financial crime was facilitated, occasioned, or attributable to non-compliance
Competent authorities shall determine if non-compliance was intentional or negligent
Competent authorities shall assess if suspension or termination introduces business continuity risks
Competent authorities shall consider opinions from designated authorities on critical ICT service providers
Competent authorities shall grant financial entities necessary time to adjust contractual arrangements
Financial entities must deploy exit strategies and transition plans

sections.source_quote: Upon receiving the reports referred to in Article 35(1), point (c), competent authorities, when taking a decision as referred to in paragraph 6 of this Article, shall take into account the type and magnitude of risk that is not addressed by the critical ICT third-party service provider, as well as the seriousness of the non-compliance, having regard to the following criteria: (a) the gravity and the duration of the non-compliance; (b) whether the non-compliance has revealed serious weaknesses in the critical ICT third-party service provider's procedures, management systems, risk management and internal controls; (c) whether a financial crime was facilitated, occasioned or is otherwise attributable to the non-compliance; (d) whether the non-compliance has been intentional or negligent; (e) whether the suspension or termination of the contractual arrangements introduces a risk for continuity of the financial entity's business operations notwithstanding the financial entity's efforts to avoid disruption in the provision of its services; (f) where applicable, the opinion of the competent authorities designated or established in accordance with Directive (EU) 2022/2555 responsible for the supervision of an essential or important entity subject to that Directive, which has been designated as a critical ICT third-party service provider, requested on a voluntary basis in accordance with paragraph 5 of this Article. Competent authorities shall grant financial entities the necessary period of time to enable them to adjust the contractual arrangements with critical ICT third-party service providers in order to avoid detrimental effects on their digital operational resilience and to allow them to deploy exit strategies and transition plans as referred to in Article 28.

sections.title: Risk assessment criteria for critical ICT service providers
sections.summary: Competent authorities must consider specific risk criteria when deciding whether to suspend or terminate critical ICT third-party services. These criteria include the severity of non-compliance, whether it reveals serious weaknesses, if financial crime was facilitated, intent behind non-compliance, business continuity risks, and opinions from designated authorities. sections.source_quote: Upon receiving the reports referred to in Article 35(1), point (c), competent authorities, when taking a decision as referred to in paragraph 6 of this Article, shall take into account the type and magnitude of risk that is not addressed by the critical ICT third-party service provider, as well as the seriousness of the non-compliance, having regard to the following criteria: (a) the gravity and the duration of the non-compliance; (b) whether the non-compliance has revealed serious weaknesses in the critical ICT third-party service provider's procedures, management systems, risk management and internal controls; (c) whether a financial crime was facilitated, occasioned or is otherwise attributable to the non-compliance; (d) whether the non-compliance has been intentional or negligent; (e) whether the suspension or termination of the contractual arrangements introduces a risk for continuity of the financial entity's business operations notwithstanding the financial entity's efforts to avoid disruption in the provision of its services; (f) where applicable, the opinion of the competent authorities designated or established in accordance with Directive (EU) 2022/2555 responsible for the supervision of an essential or important entity subject to that Directive, which has been designated as a critical ICT third-party service provider, requested on a voluntary basis in accordance with paragraph 5 of this Article. Competent authorities shall grant financial entities the necessary period of time to enable them to adjust the contractual arrangements with critical ICT third-party service providers in order to avoid detrimental effects on their digital operational resilience and to allow them to deploy exit strategies and transition plans as referred to in Article 28.

sections.title: Exclusion of entities from GDPR scope by Member States

sections.title: Exclusion of entities from GDPR scope by Member States
sections.summary: Member States can exclude financial entities listed in Article 2(5) points (4) to (23) of Directive 2013/36/EU from GDPR's scope if they are located within the Member State. When excluding such entities, Member States must notify the European Commission and provide updates on any changes to the exclusions. The Commission then publishes this information publicly. This section lists entities exempt from GDPR's scope under Article 2(5) points (4) to (23). It defines key financial terms like 'insurance undertaking', 'reinsurance undertaking', and references relevant EU directives and regulations for collective investment and prudential requirements. These exclusions apply to financial institutions and entities covered by specific EU legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows Member States to exclude specific financial entities from GDPR's scope, which may impact data transfer assessments for entities that are exempt from the regulation's requirements. This section identifies financial entities that may be excluded from GDPR's scope, which is relevant for DTIAs as it clarifies which entities require specific data transfer safeguards and which may operate under different regulatory frameworks.

sections.title: Delegated acts require no objections for entry into force
sections.summary: A delegated act adopted under Articles 31(6) and 43(2) enters into force only if no objections are expressed by either the European Parliament or Council within three months of notification. If no objections are made, the act enters into force; otherwise, it does not. The three-month period may be extended by either body.

sections.title: Non-binding opinions for consistent supervisory measures
sections.summary: If a critical ICT third-party service provider refuses to endorse recommendations from the Lead Overseer and this divergence risks financial stability, the Lead Overseer may issue non-binding opinions after consulting the Oversight Forum. These opinions aim to promote consistent supervisory follow-up measures across competent authorities to address potential risks to financial stability.

sections.source_quote: Where a critical ICT third-party service provider refuses to endorse recommendations, based on a divergent approach from the one advised by the Lead Overseer, and such a divergent approach may adversely impact a large number of financial entities, or a significant part of the financial sector, and individual warnings issued by competent authorities have not resulted in consistent approaches mitigating the potential risk to financial stability, the Lead Overseer may, after consulting the Oversight Forum, issue non-binding and non-public opinions to competent authorities, in order to promote consistent and convergent supervisory follow-up measures, as appropriate.

sections.title: Risk assessment criteria for critical ICT service providers
sections.summary: Competent authorities must consider specific risk criteria when deciding whether to suspend or terminate critical ICT third-party services. These criteria include the severity of non-compliance, whether it reveals serious weaknesses, if financial crime was facilitated, intent behind non-compliance, business continuity risks, and opinions from designated authorities. Financial entities must be given time to adjust contracts and deploy exit strategies.

Competent authorities shall determine if non-compliance was intentional or negligent

sections.source_quote: Upon receiving the reports referred to in Article 35(1), point (c), competent authorities, when taking a decision as referred to in paragraph 6 of this Article, shall take into account the type and magnitude of risk that is not addressed by the critical ICT third-party service provider, as well as the seriousness of the non-compliance, having regard to the following criteria: (a) the gravity and the duration of the non-compliance; (b) whether the non-compliance has revealed serious weaknesses in the critical ICT third-party service provider's procedures, management systems, risk management and internal controls; (c) whether a financial crime was facilitated, occasioned or is otherwise attributable to the non-compliance; (d) whether the non-compliance has been intentional or negligent; (e) whether the suspension or termination of the contractual arrangements introduces a risk for continuity of the financial entity's business operations notwithstanding the financial entity's efforts to avoid disruption in the provision of its services; (f) where applicable, the opinion of the competent authorities designated or established in accordance with Directive (EU) 2022/2555 responsible for the supervision of an essential or important entity subject to that Directive, which has been designated as a critical ICT third-party service provider, requested on a voluntary basis in accordance with paragraph 5 of this Article. Competent authorities shall grant financial entities the necessary period of time to enable them to adjust the contractual arrangements with critical ICT third-party service providers in order to avoid detrimental effects on their digital operational resilience and to allow them to deploy exit strategies and transition plans as referred to in Article 28.

sections.title: Exclusion of entities from GDPR scope by Member States
sections.summary: Member States can exclude financial entities listed in Article 2(5) points (4) to (23) of Directive 2013/36/EU from GDPR's scope if they are located within the Member State. When excluding such entities, Member States must notify the European Commission and provide updates on any changes to the exclusions. The Commission then publishes this information publicly. This section lists entities exempt from GDPR's scope under Article 2(5) points (4) to (23). It defines key financial terms like 'insurance undertaking', 'reinsurance undertaking', and references relevant EU directives and regulations for collective investment and prudential requirements. These exclusions apply to financial institutions and entities covered by specific EU legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows Member States to exclude specific financial entities from GDPR's scope, which may impact data transfer assessments for entities that are exempt from the regulation's requirements. This section identifies financial entities that may be excluded from GDPR's scope, which is relevant for DTIAs as it clarifies which entities require specific data transfer safeguards and which may operate under different regulatory frameworks.

requirements.text: Member States may exclude from the scope of this Regulation entities referred to in Article 2(5), points (4) to (23), of Directive 2013/36/EU that are located within their respective territories.
Where a Member State makes use of such option, it shall inform the Commission thereof as well as of any subsequent changes thereto.
The Commission shall make that information publicly available on its website or other easily accessible means.

‘Reinsurance undertaking' means a reinsurance undertaking as defined in Article 13, point (4), of Directive 2009/138/EC

Regulation (EU) No 575/2013 sets prudential requirements for credit institutions
